perturbation.original_text: Each Station transmitting the Service shall have the right to broadcast the Service on its Primary Feed, in addition to its broadcasts under Section 3(a). Such broadcasts shall be subject to all terms and conditions of this Agreement, including Sections 4(e) and 8(c).
perturbation.changed_text: Each Station transmitting the Service may broadcast the Service on its Primary Feed, in addition to broadcasts under Section 3(a). These broadcasts will not be subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that the broadcasts on the Primary Feed are not subject to the agreement's terms, including advertising restrictions and content limitations, it contradicts the rest of the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could violate retransmission consent rules if the primary feed is altered significantly and retransmitted without proper consent, since the original agreement mandated adherence to content and advertising standards.
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly states that broadcasts on the Primary Feed will not be subject to the terms and conditions of the agreement, which directly contradicts the original text. The perturbation explanation highlights that this includes advertising restrictions and content limitations, implying a significant departure from the initial contractual obligations. While the provided law snippet is limited, the fact that the modification exempts broadcasts from 'all terms and conditions,' suggests a potential violation of retransmission consent rules as stated in the Law Explanation, especially if the primary feed is altered significantly without proper consent. This creates a legally recognized contradiction because it deviates from legally recognized definitions and obligations within the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: Network will provide as part of the Service the minimum number of hours of "Core Programming," as defined in 47 C.F.R. §73.671(c), as the same may be amended from time to time ("Core Children's Programming"), and will comply with related requirements of the definition of "Core Programming" in order to enable Affiliate to comply with the "safe harbor" established by law or FCC regulation, solely with regard to the Service and as a result of the broadcast by the Stations of the Service on each such Station's free, over-the-air, multicast feed.
perturbation.changed_text: Network will attempt to provide a reasonable amount of children's programming. However, compliance with 47 C.F.R. §73.671(c) is not guaranteed, and Affiliate is solely responsible for ensuring programming meets regulatory guidelines.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text shifts the responsibility for children's programming compliance to the Affiliate, and removes Network's guarantee to comply, thus contradicting the original intent and creating uncertainty about regulatory compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 47 CFR § 73.671 - Commercial limits in children's programs
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that compliance with children's programming rules is not guaranteed, the modified text puts the affiliate at risk of violating FCC regulations regarding commercial limits and educational content for children's television.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 47 CFR § 73.671 - Educational and informational programming for children. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 47 CFR § 73.671 - Educational and informational programming for children. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 73.671 Educational and informational programming for children. (a) Each commercial and noncommercial educational television broadcast station licensee has an obligation to serve, over the term of its license, the educational and informational needs of children through both the licensee's overall programming and programming specifically designed to serve such needs. (b) Any special nonbroadcast efforts which enhance the value of children's educational and informational television programming, and any special effort to produce or support educational and informational television programming by another station in the licensee's marketplace, may also contribute to meeting the licensee's obligation to serve, over the term of its license, the educational and informational needs of children. (c) For purposes of this section, educational and informational television programming is any television programming that furthers the educational and informational needs of children 16 years of age and under in any respect, including the child's intellectual/cognitive or social/emotional needs. Programming specifically designed to serve the educational and informational needs of children (“Core Programming”) is educational and informational programming that satisfies the following additional criteria: (1) It has serving the educational and informational needs of children ages 16 and under as a significant purpose; (2) It is aired between the hours of 6:00 a.m. and 10:00 p.m.; (3) It is a regularly scheduled weekly program, except that a licensee may air a limited amount of programming that is not regularly scheduled on a weekly basis, including educational specials and regula
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the original contract and FCC regulations regarding children's programming. The original text ensures compliance with 47 C.F.R. §73.671(c), while the modified text states that compliance is not guaranteed and shifts responsibility to the Affiliate. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from 47 CFR § 73.671, states that each station licensee has an obligation to serve the educational and informational needs of children. By removing the Network's guarantee to comply and placing the responsibility on the Affiliate, the changed text undermines the original intent and puts the affiliate at risk of violating FCC regulations regarding educational content for children's television, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. This constitutes misaligned terminology that creates a legal contradiction because the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Network shall provide full-time closed captioning for the Service in all programming and at all times for which captioning is required by applicable law as applied to the Service.
perturbation.changed_text: Network will endeavor to provide closed captioning for the Service where feasible, but the extent of captioning will depend on available resources and is not guaranteed for all programming.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall provide' to 'will endeavor to provide' weakens the obligation to provide closed captioning, contradicting the intent to comply with applicable laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 47 U.S. Code § 613 - Closed captioning of video programming
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification could lead to a violation of federal law requiring closed captioning for video programming. The original text ensured compliance with closed captioning requirements, while the modified text introduces ambiguity and potential non-compliance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 47 U.S. Code § 613 - Video programming accessibility | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 47 U.S. Code § 613 - Video programming accessibility U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Repealed. Pub. L. 115–141, div. P, title IV, § 402(i)(11) , Mar. 23, 2018 , 132 Stat. 1090 (b) Accountability criteria Within 18 months after February 8, 1996 , the Commission shall prescribe such regulations as are necessary to implement this section. Such regulations shall ensure that— (1) video programming first published or exhibited after the effective date of such regulations is fully accessible through the provision of closed captions, except as provided in subsection (d); and (2) video programming providers or owners maximize the accessibility of video programming first published or exhibited prior to the effective date of such regulations through the provision of closed captions, except as provided in subsection (d). (c) Deadlines for captioning (1) In general The regulations prescribed pursuant to subsection (b) shall include an appropriate schedule of deadlines for the provision of closed captioning of video programming once published or exhibited on television. (2) Deadlines for programming delivered using Internet protocol (A) Regulations on closed captioning on video programming delivered using Internet protocol Not later than 6 months after the submission of the report to the Commission required by subsection (e)(1) [1] of the Twenty-First Century Communications and Video Accessibility Act of 2010, the Commission shall revise its regulations to require the provision of closed captioning on video programming delivered using Internet protocol that was published or exhibited on television with captions after the effective date of such regulations. (B) Schedule The regulations prescribed under this paragraph shall include an appropriate schedule of deadlines for the provisi
perturbation.justification: The original text states "Network shall provide full-time closed captioning… for which captioning is required by applicable law", indicating a mandatory obligation. The changed text weakens this obligation significantly by stating "Network will endeavor to provide closed captioning… where feasible, but the extent of captioning will depend on available resources and is not guaranteed". This shift introduces ambiguity regarding compliance with applicable laws requiring closed captioning. 47 U.S. Code § 613 (b)(1) states regulations "shall ensure that video programming first published or exhibited after the effective date of such regulations is fully accessible through the provision of closed captions." The modified text's discretionary language directly contradicts the legal requirement for ensured accessibility. The change from 'shall' to 'endeavor' and the introduction of resource constraints create a loophole, potentially leading to non-compliance with the law, thus creating a significant legal contradiction.
